metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete signal that indicates 'already published' (e.g., a specific exit code, a grep pattern on mcp-publisher output, or a separate `curl https://registry…` probe) so the idempotency branch is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how to detect 'already published' during the retry loop — whether by exit code, a specific error-message pattern in stdout/stderr, or a separate API query to the MCP Registry. An executor cannot implement the idempotency check without knowing the observable signal.
findings.location: Fix section — 'if the version is already present in the registry, treat it as success'; AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note such as: 'Check for already-published by running `curl -sf https://<registry-url>/<package>/<version>` before (or after a failed) publish attempt; treat HTTP 200 as success and skip/stop retrying.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Probe `mcp-publisher publish` behavior on a duplicate version (or read its docs/source) to confirm the exact exit code / output pattern that signals 'already published', and document this in the implementation so the retry's idempotency check is grounded in a verified contract.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'if the version is already present in the registry, treat it as success' but does not validate or specify HOW `mcp-publisher publish` signals an already-published version (exit code, stderr pattern, or separate query) — this is an unverified assumption about an external tool's behavior that could silently break the retry logic.
findings.location: Fix section — 'Wrap mcp-publisher publish in a bounded retry … if the version is already present in the registry, treat it as success'
findings.suggested_fix: Before writing the retry shell block, run `mcp-publisher publish` against a test registry with a version that already exists and record the exit code and stderr. If it emits a distinguishable message (e.g., 'already exists'), grep for it; if it exits 0, no guard is needed; if the tool has a `--check` flag, use that as the pre-check instead of post-failure inspection.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete signal used to detect 'already published' in the registry (e.g., a specific mcp-publisher exit code, a known stderr/stdout pattern, or a registry API query URL) so AC3 is verifiable and the implementer cannot wire it to the wrong condition.
findings.finding: AC3 specifies that the retry loop 'treats an already-published version (present in the registry) as success,' but the plan does not specify the observable signal used to detect this condition — e.g., whether it relies on a specific mcp-publisher exit code, a known error message/string, or a separate registry API query. Without this, an implementer may wire the check to the wrong signal and the AC cannot be deterministically verified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC3 / Fix section – 'Wrap mcp-publisher publish in a bounded retry'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Fix section, add: 'On each retry, if `mcp-publisher publish` exits non-zero, query the registry API (e.g., `GET https://registry.example.com/servers/<name>`) and check whether the current version is already listed; if so, exit 0. Otherwise propagate the failure after N attempts.' Adjust the API endpoint to match the actual MCP Registry API.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the observable signal from `mcp-publisher publish` that indicates 'version already published' (e.g. exit code, specific stderr substring) so the retry's idempotency branch can be implemented without guessing.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that a retry after a partial publish can detect 'the version is already present in the registry' and treat it as success, but no codebase evidence exists for what signal `mcp-publisher publish` emits when a version is already published (specific exit code, stderr pattern, or HTTP status). Without knowing this contract, the idempotency logic cannot be correctly implemented.
findings.location: Fix section — 'on a failed attempt, if the version is already present in the registry, treat it as success'; AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete observable signal used to detect 'already present in registry' during the retry loop (e.g., a specific exit code, a grep-able output pattern from `mcp-publisher publish`, or an explicit registry API query to `https://registry.modelcontextprotocol.io/v0/servers?search=io.github.navapbc/rebar` that confirms the version is live).
findings.finding: The plan's idempotency guarantee — 'if the version is already present in the registry, treat it as success' — has no specified detection mechanism. The plan does not state whether 'already present' is signaled by a specific exit code from `mcp-publisher publish`, a grep-able stderr/stdout pattern, or a separate out-of-band registry API query. Without this signal, the implementer cannot correctly write the conditional branch that distinguishes 'already published (success)' from 'other failure (retry)'.
findings.location: Fix section — 'idempotency-safe … if the version is already present in the registry, treat it as success' / AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before implementing the idempotency check, verify and document the exact signal (exit code and/or stdout/stderr pattern) that `mcp-publisher publish` emits when the version already exists in the MCP Registry — e.g., by inspecting the `mcp-publisher` source/releases or testing against the registry's staging environment.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'if the version is already present in the registry, treat it as success' but makes an unverified capability claim: it is not established what observable signal (exit code, stdout/stderr pattern, or separate API check) `mcp-publisher publish` produces when the server version already exists in the MCP Registry. Without knowing `mcp-publisher`'s error contract for this case, the idempotency heuristic cannot be reliably implemented and risks masking genuine failures.
findings.location: Fix section (bullet 2) / AC line 3: 'The retry treats an already-published version (present in the registry) as success.'
findings.suggested_fix: Check the `mcp-publisher` source at https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry for documented exit codes or HTTP response handling. As a safe fallback, perform the idempotency check by querying the registry API directly (e.g., `curl -s 'https://registry.modelcontextprotocol.io/v0/servers?search=io.github.navapbc/rebar'`) and parsing the version field — this is observable and independent of `mcp-publisher`'s undocumented error contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before implementing AC3's idempotency guard, verify (via `mcp-publisher publish --help`, the MCP registry CLI source, or a test run against the registry staging environment) what exit code and/or output `mcp-publisher publish` emits when the version is already present, and document the detection mechanism explicitly in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan's idempotency guarantee (AC3: treat an already-published version as success) depends on an unverified external CLI behaviour: the exit code and/or output that `mcp-publisher publish` emits when a version is already present in the registry. The plan does not name this signal, and it is not documented anywhere in the codebase. If the CLI exits 0 on a duplicate, no special handling is needed; if it exits non-zero with a generic code, the retry loop would infinite-retry a permanent error; if it emits a distinguishable message, the shell logic must grep for it. The implementer cannot write correct shell without knowing which case applies.
findings.location: Fix section (third bullet) and AC3: 'The retry treats an already-published version (present in the registry) as success.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a spike step: run `mcp-publisher publish` against the MCP registry with an already-published version (or read the CLI source at github.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry) and record the exact exit code + output. If exit code is 0 on duplicate, remove the idempotency guard from the plan (it is not needed). If non-zero, name the exact grep pattern or exit code that distinguishes 'already published' from other failures.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Quantify the retry count and backoff schedule (e.g., '3 attempts, 30 s / 60 s / 120 s') so AC2 is objectively verifiable.
findings.finding: The retry bounds ('a few attempts') are unquantified; an executor must invent a concrete number and backoff schedule without guidance, making AC2 hard to verify objectively.
findings.location: Fix section — 'a few attempts with backoff'; AC line 2 — 'bounded number of attempts and backoff'
findings.suggested_fix: State '3 retries with 30 s, 60 s, 120 s delays' (matching the ~5 min polling budget already cited).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/releasing.md` (step 4, 'Update the MCP Registry') to the plan's edit set and describe the PyPI-poll + retry behavior in that section.
findings.finding: `docs/releasing.md` step 4 documents the `mcp_registry` job's behavior in detail (it runs a bare `mcp-publisher publish` after PyPI with no retry/poll) and will be stale after the plan's changes, but the plan does not enumerate it as an edit target.
findings.location: Plan section 'Fix' / docs/releasing.md lines 121–150 ('Update the MCP Registry — automated (OIDC in CI)')
findings.suggested_fix: In `docs/releasing.md` step 4, add a sentence after 'The job `needs: publish`, so it runs after PyPI.' noting that the job also polls `https://pypi.org/pypi/nava-rebar/<version>/json` (up to ~5 min) before publishing, and retries `mcp-publisher publish` with backoff, treating an already-present registry version as success.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC that behaviorally validates the polling and retry logic — e.g., a local script test that mocks a 404-then-200 PyPI sequence to confirm the poll loop exits correctly, OR a documented manual dry-run procedure against a test PyPI index, so that the retry/idempotency paths are verified before merge.
findings.finding: The only explicit test criterion for the new polling and retry logic is 'the workflow YAML parses' (AC4) — a purely structural check that bypasses the new runtime behavior entirely. Neither the poll timeout path, the retry-exhaustion path, nor the idempotency check (already-published = success) are validated by any AC. This is a proxy-validation gap: the test suite can go green (YAML parses, Gerrit Verified on a happy-path release) while the failure-path logic is silently broken.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'The poll-and-retry shell logic is validated locally (or in CI via a lightweight mock-server or `act`) against a simulated 404→200 PyPI sequence, confirming the loop exits on 200 and the retry treats an already-published registry entry as success.' This makes the behavioral contract checkable without requiring a real tag release.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit progress logging to the polling loop (e.g., `echo "Attempt $i: PyPI returned $status, waiting…"`) and to each retry of `mcp-publisher publish`, so the CI log provides enough context to diagnose a future failure without re-reading this plan.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify any observability (log output, step summaries, or annotations) for the polling loop or retry attempts, so if the job hangs at the poll step or exhausts retries, operators see a timeout with no context about how many polls were attempted or what HTTP status was last observed.
findings.location: Fix section — polling and retry steps

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the version derivation: specify that `github.ref_name` (`v0.7.1`) must have its `v` prefix stripped (e.g. `${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}`) to form the bare `0.7.1` needed for the PyPI JSON API URL.
findings.finding: The plan proposes deriving the version from '${GITHUB_REF#refs/tags/v} / github.ref_name' interchangeably, but these produce different strings: `github.ref_name` for tag `v0.7.1` yields `v0.7.1` (with the `v` prefix), while the PyPI JSON API URL requires the bare version `0.7.1` (e.g. `https://pypi.org/pypi/nava-rebar/0.7.1/json` — confirmed by docs/releasing.md:106). Using `github.ref_name` directly in the poll URL would always return 404, defeating the wait loop.
findings.location: Fix section — 'Derive the version from the tag (`${GITHUB_REF#refs/tags/v}` / `github.ref_name`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `VERSION=${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}` (or `VERSION=${GITHUB_REF#refs/tags/v}`) to strip the leading `v`, then poll `https://pypi.org/pypi/nava-rebar/${VERSION}/json`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either derive the 5-min PyPI poll timeout from observed propagation data (e.g., timestamps in the Actions run 28806482655 log) or document the chosen value and expose it as a named env var at the top of the workflow job so it can be adjusted without hunting through the script body.
findings.finding: The polling timeout (~5 min) and retry count/backoff for `mcp-publisher publish` are asserted without derivation from measurement, and are hardcoded into the workflow YAML with no way to tune them without editing the file.
findings.location: Fix section — 'bounded ~5 min', 'a few attempts with backoff'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a job-level `env:` block: `PYPI_POLL_TIMEOUT_S: 300` and `MCP_PUBLISH_MAX_ATTEMPTS: 5`, reference these in the shell script, and add a comment citing the rationale (e.g., 'PyPI propagation observed <60 s in run 28806482655; 300 s gives 5× headroom').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the Fix section explaining why the chosen approach (inline PyPI poll + shell retry in the existing job) fits better than alternatives on the axes of simplicity, blast-radius, and codebase alignment.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for choosing the inline-poll + bounded-shell-retry approach over alternatives. The 'Why' section explains the release reliability goal but not why this specific mechanism (polling inside the mcp_registry job + shell-level retry) is the best fit over, e.g., a dedicated upstream 'pypi-ready' job or a well-tested marketplace retry action.
findings.location: Why section / Fix section — no rationale for approach choice

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or a documented deferral rationale) for end-to-end live exercise of the polling + retry path — e.g., 'a test tag release is triggered post-merge and the mcp_registry job completes green without manual intervention, visible in the Actions run URL.'
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the PyPI-poll + retry path end-to-end against a live release; the only in-session verifiable AC is YAML parse validity. The plan gives no documented rationale for deferring a live exercise.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, AC2, AC3, AC4; Fix section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `mcp-publisher login github-oidc` occurs AFTER the PyPI readiness poll completes (or inside each retry attempt), so the OIDC token is fresh when `mcp-publisher publish` executes.
findings.finding: The OIDC token minted by `mcp-publisher login github-oidc` has a short lifetime (GitHub Actions OIDC tokens are typically valid for ~5–10 minutes). If the PyPI readiness poll runs for up to ~5 minutes BEFORE the publish step, the token obtained at login time may have expired by the time `mcp-publisher publish` executes — the plan does not specify that login should occur AFTER the poll completes, and leaves this ordering ambiguous.
findings.location: Fix section — 'poll PyPI … bounded ~5 min' + current workflow steps: login then publish (release.yml:133-136)
findings.suggested_fix: Reorder the steps: (1) PyPI poll, (2) mcp-publisher login github-oidc, (3) mcp-publisher publish with retry. Alternatively, if the retry loop covers multiple attempts, re-run login at the start of each attempt so each publish call uses a fresh token.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
